title: Python for ABAP Developers
description: Python for experienced ABAP developers — a fast-track guide to becoming productive in Python within 4 weeks. Covers ABAP to Python concept mapping (data types, control flow, OOP, error handling, collections), Python idioms ABAP developers miss, FAANG-relevant libraries (pytest, Flask, requests), and a 4-week learning plan with SAP-themed practice projects.

description.value:
Variables and Data Types: ABAP uses DATA with explicit types. Python uses dynamic assignment — name = value with no type declaration. The type is inferred at runtime. ABAP's strict typing (TYPE, LIKE, REF TO) has no direct equivalent — Python uses duck typing. Key difference: ABAP's DATA(lv_name) TYPE string becomes name = "hello" in Python. Type conversions that require MOVE or CONV in ABAP are automatic in Python.

Control Flow: ABAP's IF/ELSEIF/ELSE/ENDIF maps to Python's if/elif/else with indentation instead of ENDIF. Python uses no parentheses around conditions and no terminating keyword. CASE/WHEN in ABAP maps to match/case in Python 3.10+. Loops: DO n TIMES/ENDDO becomes for _ in range(n):. WHILE/ENDWHILE becomes while condition:. LOOP AT/ENDLOOP becomes for item in iterable:.

Internal Tables to Lists: ABAP's STANDARD TABLE with INDEX is Python's list. APPEND = .append(), INSERT = .insert(index, value), DELETE = .pop() or del list[index]. READ TABLE WITH KEY becomes list comprehension or filter().

Structures and Tables to Dictionaries: ABAP's STRUCTURE + TABLE pair maps to Python's dict. A SORTED TABLE with UNIQUE KEY is equivalent to a dict (hash map with O(1) lookup). Python's dict preserves insertion order (as of 3.7), similar to STANDARD TABLE with INDEX.

Methods to Functions: ABAP's METHOD/ENDMETHOD with IMPORTING/RETURNING maps to def function(params) -> return_type:. Python functions are first-class objects — you can pass them as arguments, unlike ABAP's method references (which are more verbose).

sections.title: Python Data Structures for ABAP Developers

description.value:
List (like STANDARD TABLE with INDEX): Python lists are ordered, mutable, and indexable — exactly like ABAP's STANDARD TABLE with INDEX. my_list.append(item) = APPEND, my_list[i] = READ TABLE INDEX i, my_list.sort() = SORT BY. Lists can hold mixed types (unlike ABAP), can be nested (list of lists), and support slicing: my_list[1:3] extracts elements 1 to 2.

Dict (like SORTED TABLE with UNIQUE KEY): Python dicts are key-value maps with O(1) average lookup — replacing ABAP's SORTED and HASHED tables. my_dict[key] = READ TABLE WITH KEY. Dicts maintain insertion order (Python 3.7+), iterate fast, and support dict comprehensions: {k: v for k, v in items if v > 0}. Use .get(key, default) to avoid KeyError — equivalent to checking sy-subrc after READ TABLE.

Set (like HASHED TABLE with UNIQUE KEY without data): Python sets are unordered collections of unique elements — the closest ABAP equivalent is a HASHED TABLE where you only care about whether a key exists. Set operations like union (|), intersection (&), and difference (-) are O(len(s)) on average. Use sets for fast membership testing or deduplication — replaces SORT + DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES.

Tuple (like an immutable ABAP STRUCTURE): Python tuples are ordered, immutable sequences — like an ABAP structure that you cannot MODIFY. Use tuples for fixed collections of related values (coordinates, RGB colors, database row snapshots). Tuples can be used as dict keys (lists cannot).

These four data structures replace almost all ABAP internal table variants. A key insight: Python's collections module provides specialized alternatives — defaultdict (auto-initializing dict like CLEAR with initial values), Counter (frequency counting like a HASHED table with COLLECT), and deque (double-ended queue for FIFO/LIFO operations replacing a STANDARD TABLE used as a queue).

sections.title: OOP in Python — ABAP Developers Guide

description.value:
Python's object model is simpler than ABAP's. There is no separate definition/implementation split — the CLASS definition contains everything. There is no CLASS-DATA section — instance and class variables are defined directly in the class body. There is no METHODS declaration — methods are defined with def inside the class. The constructor is __init__ — called automatically when you create an object (like CONSTRUCTOR in ABAP).

Inheritance: Python uses class Child(Parent): — no INHERITING FROM. Multiple inheritance is supported (unlike ABAP's single inheritance with INTERFACES). Python uses super().__init__() to call the parent constructor — equivalent to super->constructor( ).

No explicit Interfaces: Python uses duck typing — "if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck." Instead of defining an interface (IF_SOME_INTERFACE) and implementing it, you just write a class with the expected methods. This is more flexible but requires discipline. For formal contracts, Python provides ABC (Abstract Base Classes) from the abc module.

Properties: Python's @property decorator replaces ABAP's GET/SET methods. Instead of METHOD get_name. rv_name = mv_name., you write @property def name(self): return self._name. This is cleaner and more Pythonic.

Magic Methods: Python uses dunder methods (__str__, __repr__, __len__, __eq__) to define object behavior — equivalent to implementing ABAP interfaces like IF_SERIALIZABLE, IF_COMPARABLE. These allow your custom objects to behave like built-in types.

sections.codeBlock:
"""ABAP CLASS vs Python class side-by-side"""

" ABAP version:
" CLASS zcl_order DEFINITION.
"   PUBLIC SECTION.
"     METHODS constructor IMPORTING iv_id TYPE string.
"     METHODS get_total RETURNING VALUE(rv_total) TYPE i.
"   PRIVATE SECTION.
"     DATA mv_id TYPE string.
"     DATA mv_total TYPE i.
" ENDCLASS.
" CLASS zcl_order IMPLEMENTATION.
"   METHOD constructor.
"     mv_id = iv_id.
"     mv_total = 0.
"   ENDMETHOD.
"   METHOD get_total.
"     rv_total = mv_total.
"   ENDMETHOD.
" ENDCLASS.

# Python equivalent:
class Order:
    def __init__(self, order_id: str):
        self._order_id = order_id
        self._total = 0

    def get_total(self) -> int:
        return self._total

# Usage is identical:
# DATA(lo_order) = NEW zcl_order( '123' ).
lo_order = Order('123')

# Key differences:
# - No separate DEFINITION / IMPLEMENTATION split
# - No DATA declarations in class body (assign in __init__)
# - No METHODS keyword (def inside a class = method)
# - No ENDCLASS (indentation defines block boundaries)
# - Constructor is __init__ (called automatically)
# - self replaces me / instance reference
# - Private by convention (_prefix) not enforced by language

description.value:
ABAP uses TRY ... CATCH ... ENDTRY with exception class hierarchies (CX_STATIC_CHECK, CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK, CX_NO_CHECK). Python uses try ... except ... finally with a simpler model. The core concepts are the same — attempt an operation, handle failures, clean up resources — but the syntax and idioms differ significantly.

Catching by type: ABAP's CATCH cx_static_check INTO lo_ex. maps to except ValueError as e:. Python catches by exception type, can catch multiple types in one block (except (ValueError, TypeError):), and can chain exceptions using raise ... from — equivalent to CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK with PREVIOUS exception.

else clause: Python's try ... except ... else runs the else block only if no exception occurred — useful for code that should not be inside the try block (avoiding accidental exception suppression). ABAP has no direct equivalent — you would need an additional flag variable.

finally clause: Both ABAP and Python have cleanup mechanisms that run whether or not an exception occurred. ABAP's CLEANUP block in TRY/CATCH maps to Python's finally:. Python's finally is more commonly used and more predictable in execution order.

Context managers: Python's with statement (with open('file.txt') as f:) automatically handles resource cleanup — equivalent to ABAP's TRY ... CATCH ... CLEANUP ... ENDTRY pattern for file/database operations. The with statement is a Python idiom that ABAP developers should embrace: it eliminates the need for explicit resource cleanup blocks.

When interviewing at FAANG or building FAANG-style projects, these Python libraries are essential:

pytest — The standard testing framework. ABAP developers used ABAP Unit (CL_AUNIT_ASSERT). pytest is simpler: write test functions (not classes), use assert (no ASSERT_EQUALS), and leverage fixtures for test setup. Pytest's parameterized tests (@pytest.mark.parametrize) reduce boilerplate compared to ABAP Unit's METHODS loop. Combined with pytest-cov for coverage, this replaces SAP's Code Inspector + ATC combination for test quality.

Flask / FastAPI — Microframeworks for building REST APIs. ABAP developers built OData services with SAP Gateway / RAP. Flask maps routes to functions with decorators: @app.route('/api/orders') vs ABAP's IWPRC method registration. FastAPI adds automatic OpenAPI documentation, Pydantic-based request validation, and native async support. For FAANG system design interviews, being able to prototype a working API in 10 lines of Flask demonstrates full-stack engineering thinking.

requests — HTTP client library that ABAP developers will appreciate for its simplicity. ABAP's cl_http_client requires 15+ lines for a simple GET request (create client, set method, set URI, send, receive, parse response). Python's requests.get(url, headers=h, timeout=5) is one line. Understanding requests.Session (connection pooling) maps directly to RFC connection pool concepts that ABAP developers already know from SM59 destinations.

pandas — Data manipulation library for data engineering and analysis. ABAP developers process data with internal tables and LOOP/AT constructs. pandas provides DataFrame (like an internal table on steroids) with vectorized operations — df.groupby('material').sum() replaces 30 lines of ABAP aggregation code using COLLECT or LOOP/SUM. Essential for data engineering interview rounds at FAANG.

Week 1: Python Basics — Variables, data types (int, float, str, bool, None), operators, conditionals (if/elif/else), loops (for, while), string operations, basic input/output. Practice: Rewrite an ABAP report that reads MARA data and prints material numbers — do the same in Python with a hardcoded list. 3 exercises: (1) FizzBuzz in Python, (2) Calculate material group totals from a list of dictionaries, (3) Parse a date string and reformat it.

Week 2: Data Structures and Functions — Lists, tuples, dicts, sets, list/dict comprehensions, function definitions, default parameters, *args/**kwargs, lambda functions, map/filter/reduce. Practice: Build a Python dict mirroring an ABAP SORTED TABLE with material-to-description mapping. Implement a function that finds duplicates (replacing SORT + DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES). 4 exercises: (1) Merge two sorted lists, (2) Word frequency counter using dict, (3) Simple calculator with lambda dispatch, (4) Convert ABAP-like table structure to list of dicts.

Week 3: Modules, File I/O, Error Handling — Importing modules, reading/writing files (CSV, JSON), try/except/else/finally, with statement (context managers), raising exceptions, basic debugging with pdb. Practice: Read an SAP IDoc-like CSV file, validate records, skip invalid ones, write valid records to a new CSV. 3 exercises: (1) Safe integer parser handling ValueError, (2) Read JSON config and print keys, (3) CSV processing script handling missing fields gracefully.

Week 4: Capstone Project — Build a CLI tool that reads a CSV of sales orders, validates data, computes aggregate metrics (total by customer, average order value, top products), and outputs a formatted report. Requirements: Use pytest for unit tests, argparse for CLI arguments, and type hints throughout. Extensions: Add Flask endpoint to serve the report as JSON, add caching with functools.lru_cache, write a GitHub Actions CI pipeline. This project demonstrates to FAANG interviewers that you can build production-quality Python code following software engineering best practices.

details: Staff+ Insight: Python is the easiest language for an ABAP developer to learn because both are interpreted, dynamically-typed, and readable. The hardest part is unlearning ABAP verbosity: Python uses indentation instead of END statements, and you don't need to declare types. Don't fight Python's idioms — embrace them. The fastest way to learn: rewrite an ABAP program you know well (a report, a function module) in Python. You'll learn more in that weekend than in any tutorial. Also: Python is the most tested language in FAANG coding interviews — it's worth investing in.
